Upcoming Events

A complete listing of events for the coming months can be found on the inside back page. Here are two of the events happening next.

RAMBO COMING

Overview:
__PH_1__ Forty controls are spread out in the park
__PH_3__ Point Defiance Park, Tacoma

More Info:

Competitors are encouraged to compete as two-person teams but single individuals may also compete. Maps will be distributed on arrival so competitors can plan optimum routes.

Thickets! Deadfall! Remote rootstocks! Swamps! Wet underbrush! Slippery trails! Snow in the air! Is this fun? The annual mid-winter, non-violent Rambo competition will be held at Point Defiance Park in Tacoma on Saturday, January 16th. There will be a mass start at 10:30 am sharp.

Competitors try to locate as many as possible in the time limit. The speedy and skilled are usually able to find all the controls within the allowed time. The Rambo trophy awaits the top team.

Driving instructions:

From I-5 take exit 132 and follow SR 16 to the 6th Ave exit. Get off SR 16 there, turn left on sixth ave and almost immediately right on Pearl St. Follow Pearl until it ends at the park entrance. Then follow O signs.

The Big St Denny Goat

A Goat? What's that?

__PH_0__ Where: Big Finn Hill Park, St Edward Park, O O Denny Park
__PH_2__ Mass Start at 10:30 am

More Info:

In the tradition of such fine events as the Billygoat, the Rocky Mountain Goat, and the Possum Trot, a Goat is coming to Puget Sound: The Big St. Denny Goat will take place on Saturday, March 6th at Big Finn Hill Park, St. Edward Park, and O.O. Denny Park in Kirkland. Check the Orienteering Hotline (206-783-3866) for details.

A Goat is a long, mass-start event in a typical point-to-point style (not score-O). (Some of you will remember the Goat that John Rogers put on a few years ago at Cle Elum Ridge.) That's a Goat event in a nutshell, but there are a few other distinguishing characteristics of this pedigree of O-event.

In most orienteering events, following is taboo, but in Goats it's encouraged. Also, Goat courses have some special strategy elements: Runners are usually allowed to skip a control or two, and there is often a forked control, where runners can take the left or the right control—it's a different kind of route choice!

Details of the Big St. Denny Goat will be on the Hotline and the O-West e-mail list.

Schedule:
Saturday 6 March Big St. Denny Goat Big Finn Hill/St. Edward/O.O. Denny, Kirkland Mass Start: Tentatively at 11 a.m. COC Eric Bone 206-634-2279

Huskies Host Training at Hamlin Park

On Sunday, March 28, the Husky Orienteering & Running Club will host a training day at Hamlin Park. There will be training on logistical tasks of hosting orienteering meets, such as registration, start, and finish timing; a course setting workshop; and orienteering skills training courses.
Register from 1:30; the activities will run from 2-3:30 p.m. Contact Eric Bone 206-634-2279 with any questions.

Copyright 1999, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club. Submission for the next issue must be received by __PH_0__. Send submissions to Don Denbo at dwd@halcyon.com or mail to 21328 N 186th St., Shoreline, WA 98133

Cascade Orienteering Club

President's Remarks

By Rick Hood

Thanks to everyone who made 1998 such a great year for orienteering locally. At the top of the list is Rick Breseman, who served so well as meet director for the USOF champs we hosted. Of course Eric Bone and Don Denbo, as course setters, and Bill Cusworth, heading up registration, also did a first class job. (There was literally a cast of dozens supporting these folks, and that cast probably included you!)

While all this was going on, COC and the other regional clubs continued to host some great local events. A special THANKS to all who helped, or assumed a leadership role for these "C" meets, the backbone of orienteering.

There was also a "behind the scenes" crew that handled the less than exciting, but ever so critical administrative tasks. A special "thumb compasses up" to outgoing club treasurer Sandy Kish Miller and outgoing board members Don Denbo, Jim Siscel, and Bill Cusworth, who will be heading up different orienteering projects this year. (To the other club officers, board members, and committee coordinators, THANK YOU on behalf of everyone.)

I was pleased to bestow the year's "President's Award" on Mike Fritz and Rick Breseman. Both have made many meaningful contributions to the club this year, that have been noted in past newsletters. (Some say it is traditional to buy them a beer the next time you see them, though I think that is a rumor Rick might have started.)

Lastly, I would like to further recognize the club's 1998 Life Service Award recipient, Don Atkinson, who has done many meet and administrative tasks for the club. As many of you might know, Don has also served as Equipment Coordinator since the mid-1990s and has done an outstanding job!

Now, as for this year, we have some exciting times ahead. We are hosting the WIOL Champs at Magnuson in February, a one-day B meet at Cle Elum in June, two of the four-day A meet in the Cle Elum/Ros Elum area this July, a "USGS Forest Rogaine" and Adventure Run toward the end of the year, and capping it all off with a two-day B meet on the last day of 1999 and the first day of 2000.

Of course all of this and other events hinge upon having volunteers to help with the meets. To this end, and as a small way of saying "thank you" we have adopted what we believe is a great idea, that Columbia River OC pioneered, called "COC Dollars". Basically you earn a COC dollar, that may be used for any COC events, newsletter subscriptions, or dues, for every full hour you help out. For example, meet staff may earn up to two COC dollars at a local event. (If you elect not to cash them in, you may use them as special drawing tickets at next year's annual dinner, for some great prizes.)

Here are a few of the other things we are working on: a shorter website address, that would be a lot easier to pass along to friends and have on the hotline, for Howard Halter's excellent website; trail project support for a local Boy Scout Troop at St. Edward, which Jim Siscel is heading up; a continued presence in local magazines and newspapers promoting the fun of orienteering at both the recreational and competitive levels, thanks to Eileen Bresman; and updating local maps so that both orienteers and course setters alike look forward to the event.

Of course, you may have seen a need for something else or have a task you would like to take on. To that, on behalf of the club I say GREAT. Please just send me an email at rickhood@gte.net or call me at 425-640-8134, so we can take the discussion to the next level!

So again, thanks for keeping orienteering both alive and well here in the greater Seattle area this past year and, in advance, thanks for your support and involvement this upcoming year.

Best wishes,
Rick

THE NEW ST EDWARD ADVENTURE RUN

Thanks to all those that came and ran on a very wet day on the steep trails of St. Edward State Park in the greater Seattle area.

Especially thanks to Sandy Kish Miller and Scott Donald for ably handling the finishes and Mark Howlett for helping with marker pick-up. (The rest of the activities were jointly handled by Bill Cusworth and Rick Hood.)

Of course, many, including myself, would say the true champion of the day was Bruce McAlister for both hosting the annual pot luck at his home and providing so many excellent food offerings!!!

"Self-Defined" Course

Eric Riggers
The Westin Family
Bob Reddick
The Davis Group
The Enger Family

Comments:

The event went very well and most orienteers enjoyed the change of pace option of an adventure run, that allow them to run trail, off-trail, or a combination of both. The club will likely host a larger adventure run next fall and make an effort to get the word out to the road and trail running clubs and community.

Prior to this year's race, the meet organizers asked: Mark Howell if he would be willing run the course exclusively on the trail; Eric Bone to run cross-country/off-trail wherever reasonable; and Jan Urban to make the "best route choice". We did this to get a sense of how trail or road runners might fare when compared to orienteers. Based upon Mark's, Eric's, and Jan's performances and their individual strengths, we were pleased with the nature of the course and the interest it generated.

If asked, we would encourage other clubs to consider offering some of these cross-over type events, as our small scale experiment seemed to go well overall. (We of course learned a thing or two from the event, that we will most certainly take advantage of next fall.)

So on behalf of Bill Cusworth, the other Co-Meet Director/Course Setter/Marker Pick-up, who might also post some comments, THANKS to everyone for trying something a little different.

Happy New Years and best wishes for a great year of O and of life,

Rick

Trail Work at St. Edward State Park

COC will continue to work on trails in St. Edward State Park during March. We will be assisting an Eagle Scout candidate from Troop 304 in Lynnwood on his Eagle project. The work will be on the South Canyon trail. This is a trail that COC re-routed several years ago out of the canyon bottom to up on the hillside. The work will involve building a bulwark on the outside of the trail, recutting the inside of the trail, and leveling the trail. Please contact Jim Siscel at 425-778-7202 later in January for the exact date if you are interested in helping.

GriRizly - Or My First Mountain Bike-O

SACO held the first "Mountain Bike-O" at Horn Rapids on November 14. This is taken from the comments of one participant

Borrowed from Frank Skorina

I'd been reading about this event called a TrailQuest in some of my mountain bike magazines and on the internet. I'm always on the lookout for "off the beaten track" type of events (hence my desire to be mountain biking in the first place) and TrailQuesting sounded like a lot of fun. For those that don't know, a TrailQuest is orienteering on a mountain bike. Almost all of these events are held in countries other than the US (the U.K. being the country I'd read the most about). Very few events of this type have been held in the US (you could probably count the total number on one hand). I had unfortunately missed the earlier event so was eager to "find some controls" before the orienteering season shut down around here for the winter.

When I got out to the event site, I was greeted by a couple of Sacajawea Orienteers members. In exchange for a small fee, I was handed a map of the Horn Rapids County Park. This orienteering map had contours, roads&trails, and other site features (rocks/trees/structures) indicated on it. It also had the general location of the orienteering "controls" that I had to find. My mission was to locate the correct controls and punch my map (using a coded punch tool on the control) when I had located them. I was an orienteering virgin and while the experienced orienteers kindly walked me through some of the map symbols, I wasn't able to keep track of what they all meant. After getting "Poke" (my long-suffering bike) all ready, I took off down the doubletrack. It was hard to keep track of all the intersections since I tried to ride with the map in my jersey pocket. It took me a little while to figure out where the first control was (I thought that this would be a real fiasco) but using what I'd learned there, I was able to continue around the course and locate the rest of the Bike-O controls. I quickly figured out that what I needed to do after finding a control was take some time to read my map and figure out what site features I could use to get me near the next control.

All was proceeding fairly good, although it was taking me a lot longer than I thought it would, until I got to the final three controls. I missed a crucial intersection on my map and went way south of where I needed to be. I spent several minutes running around on foot and on the bike trying to locate myself. When I finally found the #11 control, I had lost a lot of time. Control #12 was found without too much trouble but when I went for the last control, I again became dis-oriented cause I still hadn't realized that I was going past the intersection. After much wandering back and forth mixed in with some frantic staring at my map, I found the doubletrack I needed to be on and eventually my last control.

I learned several things from my first try at MTB Orienteering:
1) MTB Orienteering is fun!
2) Orienteering is a pretty good workout
3) I stink at reading a map (just ask my wife)
4) It would help a whole bunch to know what all the orienteering symbols mean (the symbols help you to narrow down your search area once you get in the general vicinity of the control)
5) I need to get a handlebar map holder

A couple hours outdoors in decidedly spring-like weather for the middle of November... 16.2 miles of riding... and a new bicycling experience combined for a very good day. Looking forward to the next event. Hope to see some more MTBers out there next time.

== Kevin Axt Kennewick, WA

Meet Results and Standings

VOLUNTEER PARK

November 7, 1998

We had a great turnout for the Volunteer Park meet held November 7th. The Post Intelligencer newspaper photographer and reporter were given insight and time to try out the sport.

The weather & scenery could not have been better - a crispy cold, sunny day with golden leaf-covered ground everywhere. The venue allowed for fast times. Many new faces came out and some enthusiasts helped themselves to a second course!

On a beautifully cold, wet day in the Northwest some 85 members of the public attended this meet, including a group of 14 youngsters with their adult leaders from Seattle Inner City Outings.

The weather was drizzly for most of the event, but that certainly didn't dampen the spirits of those who attended. A large group of WIOL students were also in attendance for their first mee. Even the large turkey who wandered over from the nearby animal farm had a good time. It went out on the Advanced Course but unfortunately DNF'd.

Northwesterners Well Represented at Big Basin

The Run in the Redwoods meet was held November 14/15 in Big Basin State Park, near Santa Cruz, California. The weather was sunny, but cold with few spots of sunlight reaching the ground through the dense canopy of the Redwoods. Meet organization was great. First day included a shuttle to the upper area of the park. The running was STEEP, open mixed with patches of green and some redwoods downed that made serious obstacles if not planned for. The map had been revised and updated since the '96 meet. Day two held equally challenging terrain. The dinner at Scoppazi's was enjoyable, with course reviews following.

Scouts Install Permanent Orienteering Courses at St. Edward State Park

There are now four suggested permanent orienteering courses at St. Edward State Park. COC is pleased to work with the Ranger Joel Pillar to insure that there is minimal environmental impact on the park. In keeping with the parks on trail preference Jim Siscel and Rick Hood placed the markers adjacent to trails or open areas of the park. It is hoped that this will encourage and widen the interest to newcomers of orienteering.

In November Eagle Scout candidate Nicholas Boos and 20 Scouts and adults from Troop 304 in Lynnwood installed twenty permanent markers. The four courses vary in difficulty from beginning to advanced orienteering level. Black and white maps and course descriptions should be ready at the park office by the end of January. All courses start and finish at the information shelter west of the Park office.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff", and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America". But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organisation that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information. It is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidises our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine alone is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade and WSOA is an IRS Section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations are tax deductible. JOIN NOW.

Bearing 315 is Published by:

A complete listing of events for the coming months can be found on the inside back page. Here are two of the events happening next.
Overview: Rambo is a three-hour score-O competition. Forty controls are spread out in the park Mass start at 10:30, Saturday, January 16 Point Defiance Park, Tacoma
Competitors are encouraged to compete as two-person teams but single individuals may also compete. Maps will be distributed on arrival so competitors can plan optimum routes.

Thickets! Deadfall! Remote rootstocks! Swamps! Wet underbrush! Slippery trails! Snow in the air! Is this fun? The annual mid-winter, non-violent Rambo competition will be held at Point Defiance Park in Tacoma on Saturday, January 16th. There will be a mass start at 10:30 am sharp.
Competitors try to locate as many as possible in the time limit. The speedy and skilled are usually able to find all the controls within the allowed time. The Rambo trophy awaits the top team.

From I-5 take exit 132 and follow SR 16 to the 6th Ave exit. Get off SR 16 there, turn left on sixth ave and almost immediately right on Pearl St. Follow Pearl until it ends at the park entrance. Then follow O signs.

What: The Big St Denny Goat Where: Big Finn Hill Park, St Edward Park, O O Denny Park When: Saturday, March 6, 1999 Mass Start at 10:30 am

In the tradition of such fine events as the Billygoat, the Rocky Mountain Goat, and the Possum Trot, a Goat is coming to Puget Sound: The Big St. Denny Goat will take place on Saturday, March 6th at Big Finn Hill Park, St. Edward Park, and O.O. Denny Park in Kirkland. Check the Orienteering Hotline (206-783-3866) for details.

A Goat is a long, mass-start event in a typical point-to-point style (not score-O). (Some of you will remember the Goat that John Rogers put on a few years ago at Cle Elum Ridge.) That's a Goat event in a nutshell, but there are a few other distinguishing characteristics of this pedigree of O-event.

In most orienteering events, following is taboo, but in Goats it's encouraged. Also, Goat courses have some special strategy elements: Runners are usually allowed to skip a control or two, and there is often a forked control, where runners can take the left or the right control—it's a different kind of route choice!
Details of the Big St. Denny Goat will be on the Hotline and the O-West e-mail list.
Schedule: Saturday 6 March Big St. Denny Goat Big Finn Hill/St. Edward/O.O. Denny, Kirkland Mass Start: Tentatively at 11 a.m. COC Eric Bone 206-634-2279
Huskies Host Training at Hamlin Park

On Sunday, March 28, the Husky Orienteering & Running Club will host a training day at Hamlin Park. There will be training on logistical tasks of hosting orienteering meets, such as registration, start, and finish timing; a course setting workshop; and orienteering skills training courses. Register from 1:30; the activities will run from 2-3:30 p.m. Contact Eric Bone 206-634-2279 with any questions.

Copyright 1999, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club. Submission for the next issue must be received by February 23, 1999. Send submissions to Don Denbo at dwd@halcyon.com or mail to 21328 N 186th St., Shoreline, WA 98133

Thanks to everyone who made 1998 such a great year for orienteering locally. At the top of the list is Rick Breseman, who served so well as meet director for the USOF champs we hosted. Of course Eric Bone and Don Denbo, as course setters, and Bill Cusworth, heading up registration, also did a first class job. (There was literally a cast of dozens supporting these folks, and that cast probably included you!)

While all this was going on, COC and the other regional clubs continued to host some great local events. A special THANKS to all who helped, or assumed a leadership role for these "C" meets, the backbone of orienteering.

There was also a "behind the scenes" crew that handled the less than exciting, but ever so critical administrative tasks. A special "thumb compasses up" to outgoing club treasurer Sandy Kish Miller and outgoing board members Don Denbo, Jim Siscel, and Bill Cusworth, who will be heading up different orienteering projects this year. (To the other club officers, board members, and committee coordinators, THANK YOU on behalf of everyone.)

I was pleased to bestow the year's "President's Award" on Mike Fritz and Rick Breseman. Both have made many meaningful contributions to the club this year, that have been noted in past newsletters. (Some say it is traditional to buy them a beer the next time you see them, though I think that is a rumor Rick might have started.)

Lastly, I would like to further recognize the club's 1998 Life Service Award recipient, Don Atkinson, who has done many meet and administrative tasks for the club. As many of you might know, Don has also served as Equipment Coordinator since the mid-1990s and has done an outstanding job!

Now, as for this year, we have some exciting times ahead. We are hosting the WIOL Champs at Magnuson in February, a one-day B meet at Cle Elum in June, two of the four-day A meet in the Cle Elum/Ros Elum area this July, a "USGS Forest Rogaine" and Adventure Run toward the end of the year, and capping it all off with a two-day B meet on the last day of 1999 and the first day of 2000.

Of course all of this and other events hinge upon having volunteers to help with the meets. To this end, and as a small way of saying "thank you" we have adopted what we believe is a great idea, that Columbia River OC pioneered, called "COC Dollars". Basically you earn a COC dollar, that may be used for any COC events, newsletter subscriptions, or dues, for every full hour you help out. For example, meet staff may earn up to two COC dollars at a local event. (If you elect not to cash them in, you may use them as special drawing tickets at next year's annual dinner, for some great prizes.)

Here are a few of the other things we are working on: a shorter website address, that would be a lot easier to pass along to friends and have on the hotline, for Howard Halter's excellent website; trail project support for a local Boy Scout Troop at St. Edward, which Jim Siscel is heading up; a continued presence in local magazines and newspapers promoting the fun of orienteering at both the recreational and competitive levels, thanks to Eileen Bresman; and updating local maps so that both orienteers and course setters alike look forward to the event.

Of course, you may have seen a need for something else or have a task you would like to take on. To that, on behalf of the club I say GREAT. Please just send me an email at rickhood@gte.net or call me at 425-640-8134, so we can take the discussion to the next level!
So again, thanks for keeping orienteering both alive and well here in the greater Seattle area this past year and, in advance, thanks for your support and involvement this upcoming year.
THE NEW ST EDWARD ADVENTURE RUN
Thanks to all those that came and ran on a very wet day on the steep trails of St. Edward State Park in the greater Seattle area.

Especially thanks to Sandy Kish Miller and Scott Donald for ably handling the finishes and Mark Howlett for helping with marker pick-up. (The rest of the activities were jointly handled by Bill Cusworth and Rick Hood.)
Of course, many, including myself, would say the true champion of the day was Bruce McAlister for both hosting the annual pot luck at his home and providing so many excellent food offerings!!!
Official Long Course Adventure Run 8 Controls, Trail Distance of 7.25 km (Based up mass start time)
Official Short Course Adventure Run 4 Controls, Trail Distance of 2.43
Eric Riggers The Westin Family Bob Reddick The Davis Group The Enger Family

The event went very well and most orienteers enjoyed the change of pace option of an adventure run, that allow them to run trail, off-trail, or a combination of both. The club will likely host a larger adventure run next fall and make an effort to get the word out to the road and trail running clubs and community.

Prior to this year's race, the meet organizers asked: Mark Howell if he would be willing run the course exclusively on the trail; Eric Bone to run cross-country/off-trail wherever reasonable; and Jan Urban to make the "best route choice". We did this to get a sense of how trail or road runners might fare when compared to orienteers. Based upon Mark's, Eric's, and Jan's performances and their individual strengths, we were pleased with the nature of the course and the interest it generated.

If asked, we would encourage other clubs to consider offering some of these cross-over type events, as our small scale experiment seemed to go well overall. (We of course learned a thing or two from the event, that we will most certainly take advantage of next fall.)
So on behalf of Bill Cusworth, the other Co-Meet Director/Course Setter/Marker Pick-up, who might also post some comments, THANKS to everyone for trying something a little different.
Happy New Years and best wishes for a great year of O and of life,
Trail Work at St. Edward State Park

COC will continue to work on trails in St. Edward State Park during March. We will be assisting an Eagle Scout candidate from Troop 304 in Lynnwood on his Eagle project. The work will be on the South Canyon trail. This is a trail that COC re-routed several years ago out of the canyon bottom to up on the hillside. The work will involve building a bulwark on the outside of the trail, recutting the inside of the trail, and leveling the trail. Please contact Jim Siscel at 425-778-7202 later in January for the exact date if you are interested in helping.
GriRizly - Or My First Mountain Bike-O
SACO held the first "Mountain Bike-O" at Horn Rapids on November 14. This is taken from the comments of one participant

I'd been reading about this event called a TrailQuest in some of my mountain bike magazines and on the internet. I'm always on the lookout for "off the beaten track" type of events (hence my desire to be mountain biking in the first place) and TrailQuesting sounded like a lot of fun. For those that don't know, a TrailQuest is orienteering on a mountain bike. Almost all of these events are held in countries other than the US (the U.K. being the country I'd read the most about). Very few events of this type have been held in the US (you could probably count the total number on one hand). I had unfortunately missed the earlier event so was eager to "find some controls" before the orienteering season shut down around here for the winter.

When I got out to the event site, I was greeted by a couple of Sacajawea Orienteers members. In exchange for a small fee, I was handed a map of the Horn Rapids County Park. This orienteering map had contours, roads&trails, and other site features (rocks/trees/structures) indicated on it. It also had the general location of the orienteering "controls" that I had to find. My mission was to locate the correct controls and punch my map (using a coded punch tool on the control) when I had located them. I was an orienteering virgin and while the experienced orienteers kindly walked me through some of the map symbols, I wasn't able to keep track of what they all meant. After getting "Poke" (my long-suffering bike) all ready, I took off down the doubletrack. It was hard to keep track of all the intersections since I tried to ride with the map in my jersey pocket. It took me a little while to figure out where the first control was (I thought that this would be a real fiasco) but using what I'd learned there, I was able to continue around the course and locate the rest of the Bike-O controls. I quickly figured out that what I needed to do after finding a control was take some time to read my map and figure out what site features I could use to get me near the next control.

All was proceeding fairly good, although it was taking me a lot longer than I thought it would, until I got to the final three controls. I missed a crucial intersection on my map and went way south of where I needed to be. I spent several minutes running around on foot and on the bike trying to locate myself. When I finally found the #11 control, I had lost a lot of time. Control #12 was found without too much trouble but when I went for the last control, I again became dis-oriented cause I still hadn't realized that I was going past the intersection. After much wandering back and forth mixed in with some frantic staring at my map, I found the doubletrack I needed to be on and eventually my last control.

I learned several things from my first try at MTB Orienteering: 1) MTB Orienteering is fun! 2) Orienteering is a pretty good workout 3) I stink at reading a map (just ask my wife) 4) It would help a whole bunch to know what all the orienteering symbols mean (the symbols help you to narrow down your search area once you get in the general vicinity of the control) 5) I need to get a handlebar map holder

A couple hours outdoors in decidedly spring-like weather for the middle of November... 16.2 miles of riding... and a new bicycling experience combined for a very good day. Looking forward to the next event. Hope to see some more MTBers out there next time.
We had a great turnout for the Volunteer Park meet held November 7th. The Post Intelligencer newspaper photographer and reporter were given insight and time to try out the sport.

The weather & scenery could not have been better - a crispy cold, sunny day with golden leaf-covered ground everywhere. The venue allowed for fast times. Many new faces came out and some enthusiasts helped themselves to a second course!

On a beautifully cold, wet day in the Northwest some 85 members of the public attended this meet, including a group of 14 youngsters with their adult leaders from Seattle Inner City Outings.

The weather was drizzly for most of the event, but that certainly didn't dampen the spirits of those who attended. A large group of WIOL students were also in attendance for their first mee. Even the large turkey who wandered over from the nearby animal farm had a good time. It went out on the Advanced Course but unfortunately DNF'd.

Northwesterners Well Represented at Big Basin

The Run in the Redwoods meet was held November 14/15 in Big Basin State Park, near Santa Cruz, California. The weather was sunny, but cold with few spots of sunlight reaching the ground through the dense canopy of the Redwoods. Meet organization was great. First day included a shuttle to the upper area of the park. The running was STEEP, open mixed with patches of green and some redwoods downed that made serious obstacles if not planned for. The map had been revised and updated since the '96 meet. Day two held equally challenging terrain. The dinner at Scoppazi's was enjoyable, with course reviews following.

Scouts Install Permanent Orienteering Courses at St. Edward State Park

There are now four suggested permanent orienteering courses at St. Edward State Park. COC is pleased to work with the Ranger Joel Pillar to insure that there is minimal environmental impact on the park. In keeping with the parks on trail preference Jim Siscel and Rick Hood placed the markers adjacent to trails or open areas of the park. It is hoped that this will encourage and widen the interest to newcomers of orienteering.

In November Eagle Scout candidate Nicholas Boos and 20 Scouts and adults from Troop 304 in Lynnwood installed twenty permanent markers. The four courses vary in difficulty from beginning to advanced orienteering level. Black and white maps and course descriptions should be ready at the park office by the end of January. All courses start and finish at the information shelter west of the Park office.
Call the Hot Line at 206-783-3866 or website at http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/pnw.html for latest information and details

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff", and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America". But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organisation that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information. It is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidises our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine alone is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade and WSOA is an IRS Section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations are tax deductible. JOIN NOW.
Cascade Orienteering Club PO Box 31375 Seattle, WA 98103 (206) 783-3866
